critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design with several thoughtful safeguards. Let me work through the genuine methodological concerns.

**Issue 1: The 14-day confirmation window creates asymmetric label availability across the chronological split**

The methodology claims labels reflect 'confirmed maliciousness within 14 days,' but the test window ends at month 18. Alerts in the final two weeks of the test window cannot have 14-day confirmation at evaluation time — they will disproportionately fall into the excluded 'unresolved' bucket or be artificially censored as negatives. More subtly, alerts near the validation/test boundary (month 15) lose their 14-day window too. This is not just a coverage issue: if malicious campaigns concentrate near these boundaries (plausible during active incident periods), the effective positive rate in the test tail will be deflated, and NDCG@20 on that portion will be measuring something different from the rest of the window. The validation set used for model selection has the same problem at the month 15 boundary, meaning the model selected may not be the one that performs best on the full confirmation-complete portion of the test set.

Evidence one way or the other: Compute positive label density as a function of alert timestamp across the full 18-month window. If there is a systematic drop in confirmed-positive rate in the final two weeks of each split boundary, the issue is real. A robustness check would restrict evaluation to alerts with at least 14 days of remaining observation time and verify that NDCG@20 rankings hold.

**Issue 2: Incident-ticket linkage as a label source introduces a feedback loop into the negative class**

An alert is positive if it is 'attached to an incident ticket ultimately confirmed malicious.' But incident ticket creation is itself a downstream product of analyst triage prioritization — analysts create tickets for what they investigate, and they investigate what their current tooling surfaces as high priority. Alerts that current tooling deprioritized (and that the new ranker would surface) are systematically underrepresented in the positive class, not because they are benign but because no analyst looked at them. The 'explicitly closed benign' negatives have the same structure: closures happen to alerts that were reviewed, and review happens to alerts that were prioritized. This creates survivorship bias in both the positive and negative label classes. The excluded 'unresolved' alerts are not a solution — they are excluded from fitting but the bias is in the labeled set itself.

This means the model is partially learning to replicate the prioritization behavior of whatever system was in place during the 18-month window, not to discover malicious alerts that were missed. A ranker trained this way will tend to reward the same alert types that previous tooling surfaced, and its NDCG@20 on the test set will partly measure agreement with historical triage rather than true maliciousness lift.

Evidence one way or the other: Compare the feature distributions of the 'excluded unresolved' alerts against the 'confirmed malicious' positives. If the unresolved set contains alerts with feature profiles similar to confirmed malicious alerts (same rules, same entity types, similar graph connectivity), that is evidence the label set is systematically missing true positives. Additionally, if the classifier trained on raw-only fields (the baseline) substantially agrees with historical analyst disposition rather than showing lift on the unresolved slice, it suggests the labels encode prior workflow rather than ground truth.

**Issue 3: The LambdaMART vs. classifier comparison does not isolate the hypothesis as cleanly as claimed**

The stated hypothesis is whether 'ranking with historical and graph context outperforms a flat alert-only classifier.' But the comparison as designed confounds two things: (a) the learning objective (ranking loss vs. binary cross-entropy with probability sorting) and (b) the feature set (full features for LambdaMART, raw-only for the baseline). The ablation structure partially addresses this — a raw-only LambdaMART is listed as a predeclared ablation — but the description assigns 'comparable hyperparameter search budget' to the two approaches without clarifying whether the raw-only ranker is in scope for that budget or whether the classifier also gets historical and graph features at any point in the ablation grid. If the raw-only classifier is never compared against the raw-only ranker, and the full-feature ranker is only compared against the raw-only classifier, the design cannot attribute gains to the ranking objective versus the features. The claim 'this isolates the hypothesis' is therefore too strong.

Evidence one way or the other: A full 2x2 design (objective: ranking vs. classification) × (features: raw-only vs. full) with all four cells evaluated on NDCG@20 would isolate both effects and their interaction. If the full-feature classifier matches or beats the raw-only ranker, the feature set is doing most of the work and the ranking objective claim is not established.

**Issue 4: Hourly triage windows as query groups may encode analyst workload patterns as a confound**

LambdaMART with hourly query groups trains the model to rank within hour-long analyst sessions. This is production-faithful in form but potentially harmful in effect: hour boundaries are administrative artifacts. Campaigns that span multiple hours will be split across query groups, potentially giving the ranker a distorted within-group signal for multi-hour incidents. More importantly, hour-window alert volumes vary dramatically (overnight quiet periods vs. morning peaks), and NDCG@20 computed within each window has very different meaning when a window contains 20 alerts vs. 200. A window with exactly 20 alerts assigns full credit to ranking all of them; a window with 200 alerts asks the model to lift the top 10% — these are not equivalent learning targets. If quiet hours dominate by count, the model will be overfit to low-volume windows that are not representative of the high-stakes morning triage scenario the metric is meant to capture.

Evidence one way or the other: Report NDCG@20 stratified by window size (e.g., <25 alerts, 25-100, >100). If performance degrades substantially in large windows — the high-pressure scenario the system is designed for — the query grouping design is misconfigured for the use case.
all_issues_raised: The 14-day confirmation window creates censored labels near split boundaries, deflating positive density in the test tail and potentially selecting a suboptimal model at validation.

Incident-ticket linkage encodes survivorship bias from prior triage tooling into both the positive and negative label classes, causing the model to partially learn to replicate historical prioritization rather than discover true positives.

The comparison between LambdaMART and the baseline classifier confounds learning objective with feature set, so the claimed isolation of the ranking-vs-classification hypothesis is overstated unless a full 2x2 ablation is run.

Hourly triage windows as query groups conflate low-volume and high-volume sessions in the NDCG@20 training signal, potentially overweighting quiet-hour windows that are unrepresentative of peak-load triage performance.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 14-day confirmation window creates censored labels near split boundaries, deflating positive density in the test tail and potentially selecting a suboptimal model at validation.

Incident-ticket linkage encodes survivorship bias from prior triage tooling into both the positive and negative label classes, causing the model to partially learn to replicate historical prioritization rather than discover true positives.

The comparison between LambdaMART and the baseline classifier confounds learning objective with feature set, so the claimed isolation of the ranking-vs-classification hypothesis is overstated unless a full 2x2 ablation is run.

Hourly triage windows as query groups conflate low-volume and high-volume sessions in the NDCG@20 training signal, potentially overweighting quiet-hour windows that are unrepresentative of peak-load triage performance.
